claim: Intelligence should be defined as skill acquisition efficiency: ability to learn new things with bounded denominators of energy and training data, not raw performance on known tasks
actionable: Design benchmarks that measure learning efficiency across domains, not task-specific performance; use human brain energy consumption as a comparison point for AI energy usage

claim: Static benchmarks are insufficient to declare AGI; AGI declaration will require interactive benchmarks where agents must explore, plan, and adapt in real-time
actionable: Focus evaluation efforts on interactive environments rather than static test sets; require agents to discover rules through exploration and demonstrate long-horizon planning

claim: Human-designed game environments prevent developer intelligence leakage into the task itself; programmatically generated games risk encoding solver patterns into the generator
actionable: Use human-designed, hand-crafted benchmark tasks rather than programmatic generation; this forces AI to reverse-engineer human intent rather than exploit generator patterns

claim: Learning efficiency is the key metric for AGI-level interactive benchmarks: tracking actions-to-goals (x-axis) vs. levels-completed (y-axis) shows learning slope
actionable: When designing interactive benchmarks, instrument for action efficiency: measure how many steps an agent takes to complete levels and compare human vs AI slopes

claim: Each new game level should introduce novel mechanics; this tests true learning rather than task-specific optimization
actionable: Structure benchmark tasks so each level/stage introduces new rules; measure agent's ability to learn and apply new mechanics on minimal examples

claim: Sparse rewards and long-horizon planning are essential constraints; minimal feedback forces real understanding rather than gradient-based pattern matching
actionable: Design benchmarks with sparse, delayed rewards that require forward planning; avoid dense reward signals that enable trivial optimization

claim: AGI benchmarks must use humans as the reference point for what general intelligence is; current AI models cannot generalize outside training data like humans do
actionable: Anchor benchmark difficulty and feasibility to human capability; if humans cannot solve it, it's not a valid generalization test

claim: Vision representation may not help LLMs on abstract reasoning tasks; multimodal encoding is optional but not necessary for strong performance
actionable: Don't assume visual representations improve LLM performance on reasoning; allow agents to use JSON/text representations; measure actual performance gains before adding vision

claim: Grok 4 achieved 16% on ARC 2; this is significant but still far from human-level performance, indicating major gaps in generalization
actionable: Use ARC 2 benchmarks to measure generalization gaps; 16% performance indicates state-of-art models lack genuine skill acquisition ability

claim: ARC V3 is designed to have durability of 3 years (36 months) before being beaten; this accounts for continued model improvements
actionable: When creating benchmarks, design for sufficient difficulty to remain relevant; expect continuous pressure from model improvements and incorporate that into timeline planning

claim: The community's best papers on ARC focus on test-time fine-tuning and adaptation; this is emerging as key technique for improving generalization
actionable: Implement test-time adaptation strategies when attempting reasoning benchmarks; allow models to refine responses based on task-specific feedback during inference

claim: AGI declaration should happen via some before-and-after moment when machines match human learning efficiency; not a spectrum but a threshold event
actionable: Design metrics to detect the inflection point where artificial systems match human skill acquisition rates; watch for discontinuous improvements rather than gradual progress

claim: Cooperation and alignment with other agents/entities in environment is a game mechanic worth testing in AGI benchmarks; prisoner's dilemma style problems measure this
actionable: Add multi-agent and cooperation requirements to reasoning benchmarks; measure how well agents model other entities and cooperate vs compete
